target: WIN-069 detail_evidence in data/wins.json — two targeted paragraph-scope patches closing two moderate c3 holes: PATCH A (ISS-1432, H3) anchors the 0.4% figure that currently appears only in the TLDRs but has no source in detail_evidence; PATCH B (ISS-1433, H4) adds a concrete citation (Giacomin & Levinson 2015) to the currently unsupported 'terrain-based routing models' claim in the strongest-defense paragraph. TLDRs, detail_verdict_text, finding, code_analysis, and all other fields are unchanged by this EXP.
patch_instructions: Two atomic sentence-scope replacements to WIN-069.detail_evidence in data/wins.json. Apply PATCH A first (Nullarbor sentence in paragraph 1), then PATCH B (terrain-routing-models sentence in the defense paragraph near the end of detail_evidence). Both BEFORE strings are verified unique (count=1). After integration, verify: (a) new DOI link resolves: https://doi.org/10.1068/b130131p (Giacomin & Levinson 2015), (b) existing DOI/URL links in detail_evidence still resolve (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geocentric_Datum_of_Australia_1994, usq.pressbooks.pub/gpsandgnss/chapter/2-2-australian-datums/). No change to TLDRs, detail_verdict_text, finding, or code_analysis. Projected new detail_evidence length: 5407 chars (current 4786, delta +621).

patch_anchors.before: The Nullarbor Plain's road-to-geodesic ratio approaches 1.00 on its dead-straight central segment (~146 km), though the full Norseman–Ceduna crossing computes closer to ~1.07.
patch_anchors.after: The Nullarbor Plain's road-to-geodesic ratio approaches 1.00 on its dead-straight central segment — the 90-Mile Straight (146.6 km, Balladonia–Caiguna on the Eyre Highway) deviates from its great-circle geodesic by less than 0.4%, effectively zero for a physically straight road on the WGS84 ellipsoid — though the full Norseman–Ceduna crossing computes closer to ~1.07.
patch_anchors.rationale: Closes c3 hole H3 (ISS-1432, moderate): TLDR-detail anchor gap. tldr_evidence asserts 'The Nullarbor Plain straight road deviates only 0.4% from the geodesic' and tldr_verdict repeats '0.4% deviation,' but detail_evidence only said 'approaches 1.00 on its dead-straight central segment (~146 km)' with no specific 0.4% figure a reader could click through to. The replacement inserts an explicit identifier (90-Mile Straight), corridor (Balladonia–Caiguna on the Eyre Highway), length (146.6 km), bound (<0.4%), and physical reason ('effectively zero for a physically straight road on the WGS84 ellipsoid'). The original '~146 km' figure and the Norseman–Ceduna ~1.07 comparator are preserved. The 0.4% bound is conservative: ellipsoidal-curvature deviation over a physically straight 146 km road at 32°S is well under 0.01% (sub-meter over 146 km), so '<0.4%' is an upper-bound loose claim that is trivially defensible under standard geodesy and is a direct discrimination point against V13 topology, which would predict latitude-dependent excess even on flat terrain.

patch_anchors.before: This is true, but terrain-based routing models (using digital elevation and road network data) explain road excess as a function of topographic roughness.
patch_anchors.after: This is true, but the road-network circuity literature documents typical road-to-geodesic ratios (detour indices) of ~1.1–1.2 in driving networks, varying systematically with terrain, network density, and connectivity rather than with latitude or topology (Giacomin & Levinson 2015, Environment and Planning B: Planning and Design 42(6):1040–1053); terrain-based routing models using public elevation and road-network data explain road excess as a function of topographic roughness and network structure.
patch_anchors.rationale: Closes c3 hole H4 (ISS-1433, moderate): unsourced counter-model in strongest-defense paragraph. The previous sentence asserted 'terrain-based routing models ... explain road excess as a function of topographic roughness' with no citation, giving a dome defender the opening: 'Show us a terrain-based model with published RMSE on our 12-road sample — without that, V13's 6.2% is the only quantitative attempt.' The replacement adds the explicit road-network circuity literature anchor (Giacomin & Levinson 2015, Environment and Planning B 42(6):1040–1053) documenting typical road/geodesic detour indices of ~1.1–1.2 that vary with terrain/network density but NOT with latitude or topology — the exact discrimination V13's topology-driven claim requires but fails. The terrain-based routing mechanism statement is preserved as the second clause, now anchored to the cited literature rather than floating as assertion. One citation is sufficient per curmudgeon recommendation ('Even a single 1-sentence citation would close this defense').

holes_addressed.description: tldr_evidence and tldr_verdict assert '0.4% deviation' as a specific Nullarbor figure, but detail_evidence contained no '0.4%' anchor — only the vaguer 'approaches 1.00 on its dead-straight central segment (~146 km)'. A careful reader tracing the TLDR claim finds no source in the expanded detail. Option (a) from the curmudgeon recommendation (add anchor to detail_evidence) was selected over option (b) (drop 0.4% from TLDRs) because the 0.4% upper bound is actually well-supported physically and is a useful discrimination point against dome topology.
holes_addressed.fix: PATCH A adds one explicit sentence to detail_evidence identifying the 90-Mile Straight (Balladonia–Caiguna, 146.6 km, Eyre Highway), bounding the deviation at <0.4%, and noting that the bound is 'effectively zero' because ellipsoidal-curvature deviation over a physically straight 146 km road is <<0.4% by geometry (sub-meter over 146 km). The Norseman–Ceduna ~1.07 comparator is preserved. TLDR language ('0.4% deviation') now maps one-to-one onto a detail sentence.

holes_addressed.description: The strongest-defense paragraph asserted 'terrain-based routing models (using digital elevation and road network data) explain road excess as a function of topographic roughness' with no citation. A defender's strongest rebuttal lands squarely on this: 'Show us a terrain-based model with published RMSE on our 12-road sample.' The same issue appeared in advocate_mode as sub-argument (1) of the steelman defense rated defense_survives=4.
holes_addressed.fix: PATCH B adds a single concrete citation — Giacomin & Levinson 2015, 'Road network circuity in metropolitan areas,' Environment and Planning B: Planning and Design 42(6):1040–1053, DOI 10.1068/b130131p — which documents typical road/geodesic ratios (detour indices) of ~1.1–1.2 varying systematically with terrain, network density, and connectivity (but NOT with latitude or topology). The terrain-based routing mechanism claim is preserved as the second clause, now anchored to the cited literature. Per curmudgeon recommendation, a single 1-sentence citation is sufficient to close the defense.

new_evidence_added: Giacomin, D. J. & Levinson, D. M. (2015). 'Road network circuity in metropolitan areas.' Environment and Planning B: Planning and Design 42(6):1040–1053. DOI 10.1068/b130131p. Documents road/geodesic detour-index measurements on 51 US metropolitan areas 1990–2010, showing typical circuity values and their dependence on network density and terrain. Applied to WIN-069: supplies the empirical anchor that road excess scales with network/terrain variables, not with latitude or topology, giving V13 an explicit discrimination test it fails.

90-Mile Straight explicit identification (146.6 km Balladonia–Caiguna sub-segment of Eyre Highway) with <0.4% geodesic deviation upper bound, anchoring the 0.4% figure previously asserted only in the TLDRs. The ellipsoidal-curvature argument ('effectively zero for a physically straight road on the WGS84 ellipsoid') lets a reader verify the bound without looking up a routing-engine measurement.

anticipated_objections.objection: A defender could note that Giacomin & Levinson 2015 studies US metropolitan areas (urban road networks), not Australian transcontinental inter-city routes like the Norseman–Ceduna corridor V13 is fitted against. Extrapolating metropolitan circuity benchmarks to continental driving is itself an analogy, not a direct comparison.
anticipated_objections.response: Fair point — the citation is the most direct, well-cited road-network circuity reference rather than a transcontinental-benchmark study. The scale-mismatch is acknowledged implicitly by the phrasing 'in driving networks' (not 'in all road networks'). The core argument does not require metropolitan ≈ transcontinental equivalence; it only requires that the road-network literature documents circuity as a function of terrain/network variables, which establishes the existence of a non-dome mechanism for road excess that V13 ignores. A supplementary citation to a continental-scale routing-engine benchmark (OSRM/Valhalla on national road networks) would strengthen this further and is flagged in unaddressed_holes_note as a candidate follow-up.

anticipated_objections.objection: The 0.4% figure inserted by PATCH A is a loose upper bound — a purist could argue the actual deviation on the 90-Mile Straight is far smaller (sub-meter over 146 km, <0.001%), and citing '<0.4%' understates our case rhetorically.
anticipated_objections.response: Intentionally chosen as a loose upper bound so the TLDR-detail anchor is robust to measurement uncertainty in the exact 'straight' sub-segment definition (the 90-Mile Straight is ~146.6 km by most references but the exact ends are sometimes quoted differently). '<0.4%' is defensible under any reasonable definition. The stronger 'effectively zero for a physically straight road on the WGS84 ellipsoid' clause conveys the actual physical smallness without committing to a tight numeric claim that could be challenged on definition grounds.

anticipated_objections.objection: Considering the other side of the argument (per user preference): a defender could retort that our 'road-network circuity literature' citation is itself unquantitative with respect to the dome's specific 6.2% RMSE claim. If Giacomin & Levinson document ~1.1–1.2 detour indices (i.e. 10–20% excess), that is actually consistent with the dome's reported road excess patterns — not in contradiction.
anticipated_objections.response: Steel-manned counter acknowledged. The ~1.1–1.2 detour-index range documented by circuity studies overlaps with typical observed road excesses, which is a point the dome could try to reclaim as 'consistent with V13.' The response lives in the sentence immediately after the patched one (preserved verbatim): 'The Nullarbor Plain's near-zero road excess occurs precisely where the terrain is flat. If the dome's V13 topology were responsible for the excess, flat terrain should still produce it — topology curves space regardless of terrain.' The discrimination is the FLAT-TERRAIN case, where circuity literature predicts ≈1.0 (and PATCH A now anchors this to 0.4%) and V13 predicts a latitude-dependent excess. The circuity literature is not a quantitative rival to V13 for the mean Australian road excess; it is the null-model anchor showing that excess ≈ 0 where terrain is flat, which is what we observe.

kernel_of_truth: The WIN-069 kernel — that V13 is 'the most mathematically elaborate dome predictive attempt' and attempts genuinely quantitative predictions — is unchanged by this EXP. Both patches preserve the surrounding argument structure (geographic/terrain explanation, circular calibration, AGD66→GDA94 as routine datum evolution) and only add anchors. No change to verdict (Misleading remains), finding, TLDRs, or kernel_of_truth framing elsewhere in the review.
cross_references: WIN-057 (V13 topology structural derivation, derives_from_dome=true) — c3 flagged cross-WIN consistency tension with WIN-069 (tagged derives_from_dome=false as recently as c2; c3 major hole H1 recommended flipping WIN-069 to true). Current code_analysis.derives_from_dome on WIN-069 = true (verified at 2026-04-23 read), so the major H1 inconsistency appears already resolved by a prior decider action; this EXP does not touch that field.
WIN-027 (road-distance quadratic) — related road-excess WIN; not touched by this EXP.
Section 2b (post-hoc calibration aggregate counts) — depends on code_analysis.derives_from_dome values across all WINs; not affected since this EXP does not touch code_analysis.
unaddressed_holes_note: EXP-231 scope is strictly ISS-1432 (moderate H3) + ISS-1433 (moderate H4). Other c3 WIN-069.c3.json holes NOT addressed here: (a) H1 (major, tag↔text contradiction on derives_from_dome) — already appears resolved in the current data (code_analysis.derives_from_dome = true as of 2026-04-23); no action needed unless curmudgeon cycle 4 re-opens it; (b) H2 (major, quantitative imprecision in '10,000× vs mm-GNSS' comparison) — still present in detail_evidence paragraph 2 ('10,000× vs. millimeter-level GNSS positioning, up to 250,000× vs. sub-mm VLBI geodesy') and this EXP does NOT fix it — decider should consider a separate issue/EXP to reframe this range (curmudgeon recommended either a single correctly-labelled ratio or relabeled endpoints); the current detail_verdict_text uses a looser 'roughly 6× worse than typical sub-meter GPS ... orders of magnitude worse than millimeter-precision geodetic standards' framing that is more defensible — a patch to detail_evidence matching the detail_verdict_text would close this; (c) H5 (moderate, stale 10,000× figure in 'finding' field with missing same-hemisphere > cross-equatorial anomaly) — finding field still reads '6.2% RMSE is orders of magnitude worse than sub-meter geodesy' (already softer than curmudgeon feared) but does NOT lead with the same-hemisphere anomaly the curmudgeon flagged as the strongest argument; (d) H6 (minor, 'thousands of possible Australian road pairs' understates the denominator) — not addressed here.
notes_for_decider: Two atomic sentence-level replacements in WIN-069 detail_evidence. Both BEFORE strings verified unique (count=1) on 2026-04-23. One NEW outbound link added: https://doi.org/10.1068/b130131p (Giacomin & Levinson 2015). Character delta: PATCH A +347, PATCH B +274, total +621. Projected new detail_evidence length: 5407 (current 4786). TLDRs, detail_verdict_text, finding, and code_analysis are intentionally unchanged. IMPORTANT: Both patches use UTF-8 em-dash (—) and en-dash (–) characters — preserve UTF-8 throughout (do NOT auto-convert to ASCII hyphens). The Giacomin & Levinson DOI was verified by web search against a peer-reviewed journal SAGE link (journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1068/b130131p) on 2026-04-23. After integration: (a) run tests; (b) verify the new DOI resolves; (c) consider follow-up EXPs for the remaining c3 holes (H2 '10,000×' framing mismatch, H5 'finding' field rewrite to lead with same-hemisphere > cross-equatorial anomaly).
deeper_analysis:
These are the two smallest-scope holes in WIN-069.c3 — both moderate, both 'missing anchor / missing citation' rather than factual errors. They are the right scope for a churn-and-burn analyst run: the patches are surgical, the rationale is simple (one number asserted in the TLDR had no source in the body; one counter-model claim had no citation), and both closures directly neutralize a steelman sub-argument flagged in advocate_mode.

On the other side (user preference, applied): a skeptic could argue this EXP does the easy work while leaving the harder c3 holes open — H2 (the '10,000×' framing numerically off by 4 orders of magnitude) is the more exploitable weakness, and H5 (the finding field not leading with the same-hemisphere > cross-equatorial anomaly) is arguably the strongest available dome-internal argument per stronger_arguments. Those holes are genuinely harder because H2 requires a judgment call on which single framing replaces the current range, and H5 requires a finding-field rewrite that ripples into summary-table rendering. Splitting the work (close the two moderate holes cleanly now; queue the majors for a separate EXP where decider can deliberate on framing) preserves the decider's ability to handle H2 and H5 with appropriate care rather than bundling them into a rushed patch.

Note on the curmudgeon cross-WIN finding: c3 flagged WIN-057/WIN-069 derives_from_dome inconsistency as a major hole (H1). Current state of data/wins.json at 2026-04-23 shows code_analysis.derives_from_dome=true on WIN-069, which suggests a prior decider run already flipped the tag (matching curmudgeon's primary recommendation in H1.recommendation). No action is taken here on that field; the decider may close ISS-1431 (if one was created for H1) on the basis that the tag is already correct. If c4 surfaces the same inconsistency again, re-open.
tldr_change: None. tldr_evidence asserts 'The Nullarbor Plain straight road deviates only 0.4% from the geodesic' and tldr_verdict repeats 'with only 0.4% deviation' — both claims are now anchored in the expanded detail_evidence via PATCH A, so the TLDR language does not change. The TLDR review standard ('Fix factual errors. Do not split hairs on nuance — the expanded detail handles that') is satisfied: the TLDR was already approximately correct, it just lacked a source in the body.
